pmos

Types of Business Models — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · PM Tactics — Strategy. A business model is the machine by which a company creates, delivers, and captures value — and the model you pick dictates which numbers decide whether you live. This primer maps the canon rather than arguing a house style. The scaffold is Osterwalder's Business Model Canvas, which catalogs the revenue-stream archetypes everything else is a variation on. From there it walks the six models PMs actually operate — B2B SaaS subscription, usage-based, marketplace, transactional/DTC, advertising/aggregation, and freemium/PLG — plus consumer subscription, and names the one metric each lives or dies by, grounded in the practitioner canon: David Skok's LTV/CAC spine, David Sacks on net dollar retention, a16z's bookings-vs-revenue discipline, a16z and Lenny Rachitsky on liquidity and fill rate, Bill Gurley on take rate, Ben Thompson's aggregation theory, and Tomasz Tunguz on freemium economics. Underneath the taxonomy sits one shared spine — acquire for less than you monetize, retain long enough for it to compound — and above it, the live debates: whether the Rule of 40 is a law or a vanity line, how much rake is "too far," and whether every company is becoming a subscription. Where the camps disagree, this primer flags it. 16 verified sources.

What a business model actually is — and the three things PMs conflate

Ask ten product leaders to define "business model" and you will get pricing on one side, revenue projections on the other, and a surprising amount of confusion in between. The most durable scaffold for cutting through that is Alexander Osterwalder's Business Model Canvas, which describes a business model as "a strategic management template that is used for developing new business models and documenting existing ones." Its value for a PM is not the poster — it is the forced separation of three things we routinely collapse into one: value creation (the left of the canvas: key activities, resources, partners), value delivery (the middle and right: value proposition, channels, customer relationships), and value capture (the bottom-right: revenue streams and cost structure). A "business model" is the whole machine, but the part this primer is about — the part that names your metrics — is value capture.

Osterwalder's canvas enumerates the revenue-stream archetypes everything in this primer is a variation on: "asset sale, usage fee, subscription fees, lending/leasing/renting, licensing, brokerage fees, and advertising." That list is worth internalizing because it cuts orthogonally to how products are usually categorized. Two products in the same category — say, two design tools — can run completely different capture machines: one a per-seat subscription, one a freemium funnel into self-serve, one a marketplace taking brokerage fees on template sales. Same value proposition, three different sets of numbers that decide survival. The model is the capture mechanism, not the product surface.

The reason the model matters more than the category is that the model picks the metric, and the metric picks the behavior. A subscription business that obsesses over new logos and ignores cohort decay is filling a leaky bucket; a marketplace that optimizes GMV while match rate quietly falls is inflating a vanity number; an ad business measuring registered users instead of engaged ones is, in a16z's phrase, watching a line go "up-and-to-the-right even when a business is shrinking." Every section below is really a claim about which number is load-bearing — and the recurring lesson is that the load-bearing number is rarely the one in the headline. Tien Tzuo, who coined "the Subscription Economy," puts the strategic stakes plainly: "the world is shifting from products to services," and in that shift "subscription companies know their customers. A happy subscriber base is the ultimate economic moat." Whether or not you buy the universality of that claim — a debate this primer returns to — it correctly locates the prize in the ongoing relationship, not the transaction.

B2B SaaS subscription — the retention-and-efficiency machine

Subscription SaaS is the most documented model in product, and the canon is unusually convergent on what matters. David Skok's "SaaS Metrics 2.0" reduces the entire question of viability to one line — "Can I make more profit from my customers than it costs me to acquire them?" — and answers it with two guardrails that have become industry defaults: "The best SaaS businesses have a LTV to CAC ratio that is higher than 3," and "many of the best SaaS businesses are able to recover their CAC in 5-7 months." LTV:CAC above 3 and payback inside roughly a year are the two numbers a SaaS PM should be able to recite about their own product.

But the metric that quietly dominates the others is retention, and specifically net dollar retention. David Sacks is blunt about its primacy: "Understanding retention rates of monthly cohorts, typically at months 12 and 24, is vital to the health of the business," and "The best SaaS companies have 120%+ Dollar Retention each year." NDR above 100% is the signature of a healthy SaaS machine because it means the existing book of business grows even with zero new logos — expansion outruns churn. Skok's warning is the same idea from the loss side: chasing acquisition before fixing retention means "you will simply be filling a leaky bucket." For a PM, this is the single highest-leverage reframe in the model — your roadmap's job is less to win new customers than to make the ones you have expand.

The rest of the SaaS instrument panel exists to keep founders honest about what counts as progress. a16z draws the line product teams blur most: bookings is "the value of a contract between the company and the customer," while revenue is "recognized when the service is actually provided," and ARR "should exclude one-time (non-recurring) fees." LTV is "the present value of the future net profit from the customer," and a16z flags the most common inflation directly — "A common mistake is to estimate the LTV as a present value of revenue" rather than net profit. They also separate gross churn ("MRR lost ... / MRR at the beginning of the month") from net churn, cautioning that "net revenue churn understates the losses" — a real trap, because a product with strong expansion can post flattering net churn while its logo base bleeds. Sacks adds the efficiency-of-spend lens: CAC payback is S&M divided by (MRR × gross margin), and the Magic Number is "the Net New ARR in a period divided by S&M expense from the prior period," ideally above one, with gross margin "at least 75%."

At scale, the canon's favorite single-line health check is the Rule of 40: "Growth Rate (%) + Profit Margin (%) ≥ 40%," balancing "two competing objectives: growth and profitability," popularized by Brad Feld in February 2015. It is genuinely useful as a north-star sanity check — but it is also contested, and a senior PM should hold it loosely (see the failure-modes section): critics note the line is "an arbitrary benchmark," the undefined profit-margin term invites "metric shopping," and the whole heuristic is "highly irrelevant for early-stage startups."

Usage-based / consumption pricing — when the meter replaces the seat

Usage-based pricing is best understood not as a separate species but as a mutation of subscription that re-wires the same metrics. Instead of charging per seat per month, the meter runs on consumption — API calls, compute, data ingested, messages sent. Snowflake, Twilio, AWS, and the modern data and AI-infrastructure cohort popularized it because it aligns price with value delivered and removes the friction of forecasting seats. The strategic consequence is that the load-bearing metric shifts even harder onto net dollar retention. When revenue scales with usage rather than seat count, a healthy customer's spend grows automatically as they succeed — which is exactly why the best consumption businesses post the most extreme NDR figures, well past Sacks' "120%+ Dollar Retention" benchmark. The model's whole appeal is that "Dollar Retention measures how much revenue a cohort is generating in each period relative to its original size," and consumption pricing turns customer success directly into cohort expansion.

The trade is predictability for alignment. Seat-based subscription gives you clean, forecastable ARR; consumption gives you revenue that breathes with the customer's business — up in their good quarters, down in their bad ones. That volatility is why usage-based businesses watch a16z's revenue-recognition discipline closely: with a meter, the gap between bookings (committed spend) and revenue (consumed spend) is real money, and conflating them — counting a committed contract as recognized revenue before consumption — produces the most common forecasting blowup in the category. The other failure mode is the inverse of SaaS's: a consumption product can show fine logo retention while per-account usage quietly contracts, so the leaky bucket here leaks dollars, not customers. PMs in this model earn their keep by driving "core action" frequency — the usage event the meter charges on — because in consumption pricing, engagement and revenue are the same curve.

Marketplaces — liquidity first, take rate later

Marketplaces are the model where the metrics most often mislead, because the headline number — GMV — is the one that lies most readily. a16z's marketplace primer defines GMV as "the total sales dollar volume of merchandise transacting through the marketplace in a specific period," revenue as "the portion of GMV that the marketplace 'takes'," and the metric that actually signals health — match rate, or liquidity — as "the rate at which buyers can find sellers, and vice versa." GMV can be bought with subsidies and incentives; liquidity cannot be faked. a16z's thirteen metrics — match rate, take rate, retention cohorts (user, core-action, dollar), power-user curves — exist "both to calibrate their performance and evaluate future potential."

Lenny Rachitsky, drawing on Airbnb, sharpens the hierarchy to four metrics — fill rate, bookings growth, supply growth, GMV growth — and crowns one above the rest: fill rate is "the ultimate measure of marketplace health, because it's the essence of what a marketplace is — a place people can consistently come to find what they want," while "bookings is the best way to track marketplace growth." The PM reading here is a sequencing rule: liquidity precedes monetization. You cannot rake what does not match, so the early-stage job is to drive fill rate and core-action retention on both sides before the take rate matters at all.

Once liquidity exists, take rate becomes the central design choice, and Bill Gurley's "A Rake Too Far" is the canonical warning against greed. "There is a big difference between what you can extract versus what you should extract," he writes, because "high rakes are a form of friction precisely because your rake becomes part of the landed price for the consumer." His examples have aged well: GroupOn's effective rake near 70% and Shutterstock paying creators only 30% sat at the dangerous end, while OpenTable, HomeAway, and oDesk — cutting commission "from 30% to 10%" — chose the durable one. His formula: "High volume combined with a modest rake is the perfect formula for a true organic marketplace." For a marketplace PM, the take-rate decision is not a pricing tweak; it is a strategic posture about whether you want to be undercut.

Transactional & e-commerce — the contribution-margin discipline

Transactional businesses — DTC brands, e-commerce, anything that sells a unit and recognizes revenue on the sale — feel simpler than subscription, and that simplicity is the trap. The model has no recurring contract to smooth the numbers, so every order has to stand on its own economics, and the metric that governs survival is one many operators underweight: contribution margin, not gross margin. The unit-economics canon for DTC is sharp on the distinction: "Gross margin doesn't include shipping, fulfillment, payment processing, or returns — all of which scale per order. Contribution margin does." And contribution margin is what actually funds growth: "Contribution margin funds CAC + fixed costs + profit. Below 30% you're going to struggle to scale profitably; 35–60% is the healthy range across DTC." A brand with a flattering 70% gross margin can still be unscalable once shipping, returns, and payment processing are netted out — which is precisely how venture-funded DTC brands of the late 2010s scaled themselves into insolvency on paid acquisition.

Because there is no automatic recurring revenue, the LTV side of the equation has to be earned through repeat behavior, and here the canon delivers a counterintuitive but well-supported lever: "Repeat purchase rate matters more than AOV uplift in driving LTV. A 5-percentage-point increase in repeat rate typically out-earns a 10% AOV increase." The reason is structural — the repeat customer carries near-zero acquisition cost on order two, three, and four, so repeat rate compounds where a one-time AOV bump does not. This is why the strongest transactional businesses behave more like subscription businesses than they admit: they invest in the post-purchase relationship, replenishment, and loyalty precisely to manufacture the recurring revenue the model doesn't give them for free. The same LTV:CAC discipline from SaaS applies, but with contribution margin — not revenue, not gross margin — as the LTV input.

Advertising & aggregation — monetizing attention at zero marginal cost

The advertising model inverts the usual relationship between user and customer: the user is not who pays. That inversion is the source of both its enormous scale and its perennial controversy, and the canonical lens for understanding why a handful of ad businesses became so dominant is Ben Thompson's Aggregation Theory. Thompson's argument is that in the internet era "suppliers can be commoditized leaving consumers/users as a first order priority," so the winners are "aggregators who aggregate modularized suppliers — which they often don't pay for — to consumers/users." The flywheel: "the best distributors/aggregators/market-makers win by providing the best experience, which earns them the most consumers/users, which attracts the most suppliers." Google and Facebook then integrated ad inventory with profile data, "enabling it to sell highly effective advertising." The ad model, in this framing, is what you bolt onto an aggregator once you control demand.

Thompson's follow-up, "Defining Aggregators," gives the three diagnostic traits a PM can test a business against: "a direct relationship with users," "zero marginal costs for serving users," and "demand-driven multi-sided networks with decreasing acquisition costs" — where "customer acquisition costs decrease over time; marginal customers are attracted to the platform by virtue of the increasing number of suppliers." That last property is the real moat: in a true aggregator, CAC falls as the network grows, the opposite of most businesses. He ranks them by how much supply costs they bear — Level 1 aggregators "acquire their supply," Level 2 "incur transaction costs," and Level 3 "incur no supplier acquisition costs" — with the zero-supply-cost Level 3 (Google, Facebook) being the most powerful.

Operationally, an ad business lives on two yardsticks that PMs frequently confuse. Per the ad-metrics canon, ARPU "shows how much money, on average, a single user generates over a set period of time," while eCPM "is the actual revenue earned by a publisher for every 1,000 ad impressions displayed on their app." ARPU is the demand-side monetization view; eCPM is the per-impression supply-side view. And the engagement metrics underneath them are where the model is most easily gamed — which is why a16z insists on "ratios of these metrics — e.g., DAUs-to-MAU" to read real engagement, and warns that registered users is "one of those dreaded 'cumulative' metrics that can go up-and-to-the-right even when a business is shrinking," so "our preferred user metric is active users." For an ad-supported PM, the load-bearing number is engaged time, because attention is the inventory you are actually selling.

Freemium & product-led growth — monetizing a fraction of a huge base

Freemium is the model most often adopted for the wrong reasons, because "free" looks like a marketing tactic when it is actually a demanding economic structure. The clearest teardown of why it works when it works is Tomasz Tunguz's analysis of the Dropbox S-1, which shows the model's signature shape: "2% of those users convert to paid," and yet "90% of revenue originates through self serve channels — an astounding figure for company that generated more than $1B in revenue." The reason that 2% conversion is not a disaster is the cost structure beneath it: "Dropbox spends more of its revenue on engineering than sales and marketing," capturing "the strategic advantages of the freemium go-to-market strategy." Freemium trades a brutal conversion rate for a near-zero customer acquisition cost — the free product is the funnel, and the funnel is nearly free to run.

That trade only nets out under specific conditions, and Tunguz's freemium-fit test names them: it works when "the number of potential users numbers in the tens of millions," "the marginal cost to serve a user is negligible," and "the product downloaded by a free user has a simple and straightforward value proposition." The disqualifier is decisive and frequently ignored: "If the cost-to-serve a free user is significant, the freemium model will demand lots of cash," and bluntly, "Freemium doesn't work for many different businesses." This is the model's central PM trap — teams reach for free without a market large enough to make a 2–4% conversion rate produce a real business, or with a product whose free tier is expensive to serve, and they end up funding an enormous non-paying user base out of a runway that was supposed to last. Freemium is product-led growth's purest form, but it is a bet on scale and serving cost, not a discount.

Consumer subscription & media — the retention-curve model

Consumer subscription — streaming, news, fitness, consumer apps — is subscription's harder cousin, because consumers churn for reasons no account manager can intervene on: boredom, a tight month, a competing release. The model's strategic logic is the one Tien Tzuo generalizes in Subscribed — "use subscriptions to build lucrative, ongoing one-on-one relationships with your customers," on the premise that "the world is shifting from products to services." But where B2B SaaS leans on expansion revenue to push NDR past 100%, consumer subscription rarely has meaningful expansion — a Netflix subscriber mostly can't spend more — so the entire model rests on the shape of the retention curve. The load-bearing question is whether the curve flattens to a durable plateau or decays toward zero, and the height of that plateau is the business.

This is why content and product cadence is the real growth lever in consumer subscription: churn is continuous, so the product has to continuously re-earn the renewal. The cost structure is the mirror image of freemium's — instead of a near-zero serving cost, consumer media often carries a heavy, recurring content or licensing cost that must be amortized across a retained base, which makes plateau height existential rather than merely nice. Tzuo's claim that "subscription companies know their customers" and that "a happy subscriber base is the ultimate economic moat" is most literally true here: the only defensible asset is a base that keeps renewing. For a PM, the work is relentlessly about reducing the reasons to cancel — onboarding to the habit fast, surfacing fresh value every cycle, and watching the cohort plateau the way a SaaS PM watches NDR.

The universal spine — every model runs on the same three numbers

Strip away the taxonomy and every model in this primer runs on the same three-part spine: acquire a customer for less than they are worth, monetize them, and retain them long enough for it to compound. The vocabulary changes — CAC and LTV in SaaS, fill rate and take rate in marketplaces, contribution margin and repeat rate in commerce, eCPM and engaged time in advertising — but the underlying loop is identical. David Skok's question is the universal one: "Can I make more profit from my customers than it costs me to acquire them?" His LTV:CAC > 3 and sub-year payback are stated for SaaS, but the structure travels: a DTC brand needs contribution-margin-based LTV above 3× CAC; a marketplace needs the lifetime take from a cohort to exceed the cost of acquiring both sides; an ad business needs lifetime ARPU to clear the cost of acquiring and serving the user.

Two disciplines hold across all models. The first is honesty about the numerator: a16z's insistence that LTV is "net profit," not revenue or gross margin, and that bookings are not revenue, is the most common place models flatter themselves regardless of category. The second is the primacy of retention as the multiplier on everything else — Skok's "leaky bucket," Sacks' "120%+ Dollar Retention," Lenny's fill rate, the DTC repeat-rate lever, the consumer-subscription plateau are all the same insight in different dialects: acquisition is linear and expensive, retention is compounding and cheap, and the businesses that win are the ones whose loop closes — where retained value lowers the effective cost of the next customer. A PM who can locate their model's version of these three numbers, and knows which is currently the constraint, understands the business better than one who has memorized the category-specific jargon.

How models compose and layer in practice

The taxonomy is a teaching device, not a description of how real companies are built — almost every business of scale runs more than one model at once, and the interesting strategy is in how they are layered. Amazon is the canonical example: a transactional retail business, a marketplace taking brokerage fees from third-party sellers, a subscription business in Prime, an advertising business selling sponsored placement, and a usage-based business in AWS — five capture mechanisms feeding one demand base. The canvas's list of revenue-stream archetypes — "asset sale, usage fee, subscription fees ... brokerage fees ... advertising" — reads, in hindsight, like Amazon's org chart. The strategic move is using a strong position in one model to seed another that would be hard to start cold.

The most common and most powerful layering is the one Aggregation Theory predicts: build demand first with a free or transactional surface, then monetize the aggregated demand with a higher-margin model bolted on top. An aggregator that controls users "by providing the best experience, which earns them the most consumers/users, which attracts the most suppliers" has earned the right to add advertising, a marketplace, or a subscription on top — because the expensive part, demand, is already paid for. This is why the layering usually runs in one direction: from the demand-aggregating model outward to the higher-capture ones, not the reverse. A subscription business can add a marketplace; a marketplace can add ads; an ad-funded aggregator can add a subscription. What rarely works is trying to bolt a demand-aggregating model onto a business that never controlled demand in the first place.

Why models migrate — and what forces the change

Models are not static. The defining business-model story of the last fifteen years is the migration toward recurring revenue, which Tien Tzuo calls the Subscription Economy: "the world is shifting from products to services," and companies that historically sold a unit — software licenses, cars, razors, enterprise hardware — have moved to ongoing relationships because, in Tzuo's framing, "a happy subscriber base is the ultimate economic moat." Adobe's move from boxed Creative Suite to Creative Cloud is the textbook case: same product, re-pointed capture mechanism, a transformed (and far more valuable) business. The forcing functions are usually some mix of falling marginal cost (cloud delivery made recurring access cheap to serve), investor preference for predictable recurring revenue, and the strategic value of the continuous customer relationship Tzuo emphasizes.

The more recent migration is in the opposite-seeming direction — from seat-based subscription toward usage-based consumption — driven by buyers wanting price to track value and by AI-era products whose costs scale with usage. Both migrations are the same underlying force: aligning capture with how value is actually created and delivered. For a PM, the lesson is that a business-model choice is a decision with a shelf life, not a permanent setting. The signals that a model is due to migrate are usually visible in the metrics first: seat-based pricing capping expansion that usage-based would unlock, a transactional business discovering its repeat customers behave like subscribers, an ad business whose users would pay to remove ads. Tzuo's thesis that "every company" is becoming a subscription is contested — many transactional and ad businesses remain structurally better off as they are — but the narrower claim, that models migrate when the capture mechanism falls out of step with how value is delivered, is well supported by the cases.

A decision guide — choosing or pivoting a model

There is no universally correct model, only a model that fits a specific value proposition, cost structure, and market size — so the choice is best made by interrogating those three. Start with serving cost and market size together, because they jointly decide whether free can be a strategy: Tunguz's test — "the number of potential users numbers in the tens of millions," "the marginal cost to serve a user is negligible," and "a simple and straightforward value proposition" — is really a filter for whether freemium or ad-supported models are even viable. If your serving cost per user is significant or your market is measured in thousands rather than millions, free is a trap and you should be charging directly.

From there, the questions cascade. Is value delivered once or continuously? Continuous delivery argues for subscription or usage-based, where retention compounds; one-time value argues for transactional, where contribution margin rules. Does your product create value by connecting two parties who would struggle to find each other? That is a marketplace, and fill rate precedes everything — including, per Gurley, the temptation to set a high take rate before liquidity exists. Do you control demand at a scale where attention itself is sellable? Only then is advertising a primary model rather than a desperate one. The honest version of the decision is that most teams should pick the model whose load-bearing metric they can actually move with their product, and revisit the choice when — per the migration section — the metrics show the capture mechanism drifting out of step with how value is delivered. A pivot is not failure; staying in a mismatched model is.

Where models break — failure modes and live debates

Each model has a signature way of lying to its operators, and the canon is most useful as a catalog of those traps. The marketplace trap is optimizing GMV while liquidity erodes — a16z separates GMV from "the rate at which buyers can find sellers" precisely because the first can be subsidized while the second cannot. The SaaS trap is the inverse churn flatter: a16z warns "net revenue churn understates the losses," so a product with strong expansion can mask a bleeding logo base. The freemium trap is funding a giant non-paying base without the market size or near-zero serving cost Tunguz requires. The ad trap is the vanity metric — registered users that "can go up-and-to-the-right even when a business is shrinking," per a16z. The transactional trap is mistaking gross margin for contribution margin and scaling paid acquisition into a hole. The marketplace pricing trap is the rake: Gurley's "difference between what you can extract versus what you should extract."

Several genuine debates remain unsettled, and a senior PM should hold them as open rather than decided. The Rule of 40 is the loudest: useful as a balance check, but critics call the 40% line "an arbitrary benchmark," note the undefined margin term invites "metric shopping," and argue it is "highly irrelevant for early-stage startups" — so treat it as a directional sanity check, not a target to optimize. The second is the universality of subscription: Tzuo's claim that essentially every company should become a subscription is powerful but contested — plenty of transactional and ad-funded businesses are structurally better off as they are, and forcing recurring revenue onto a fundamentally transactional value proposition (the much-mocked "subscription for everything" era) burned real capital. The third is take-rate philosophy: Gurley's low-rake doctrine is canon, but high-rake platforms with genuine lock-in have also endured, so "a rake too far" is a risk to weigh, not a hard ceiling. The throughline is that the model names the metric, the metric can lie, and the PM's job is to know which number is load-bearing this quarter — and to distrust the one in the headline.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

This primer maps the capture machine; these adjacent topics go deeper on the parts it only touched.

  • Pricing & packaging strategy — the model sets the revenue archetype; pricing and packaging decide the actual numbers (tiers, seats, meters, value metric).
  • Unit economics & LTV/CAC — the cross-cutting spine of this primer, taken to the formula level: cohort LTV construction, payback math, blended vs paid CAC.
  • Retention & cohort analysis — the load-bearing metric in nearly every model; how to read retention curves and find the plateau.
  • Network effects & marketplace dynamics — the deep version of the liquidity and take-rate material, including cold-start and multi-tenanting.
  • Product-led growth — freemium and self-serve as a go-to-market motion, not just a revenue model.
  • Platform & aggregation strategy — Thompson's theory extended into competitive strategy, moats, and regulation.
pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_types-of-business-models.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
